questions.stem: A 78-year-old man has 8 months of worsening fatigue, exertional dyspnea, and recurrent skin infections. He does not drink alcohol and has no liver disease. CBC: Hb 82 g/L, MCV 111 fL, WBC 2.6 ×10⁹/L, ANC 0.7 ×10⁹/L, platelets 48 ×10⁹/L. The film shows oval macrocytes, hypogranular hypolobated neutrophils, and rare blasts. B12, liver enzymes, and viral serologies are normal/negative, and the spleen is normal on ultrasound. What is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?
options: Start oral iron and repeat the CBC in 3 months
Reassure, as macrocytosis is common in older adults
questions.explain: Macrocytic anemia with neutropenia, thrombocytopenia, dysplastic neutrophils, and rare blasts in an older adult is highly concerning for MDS (or another marrow disorder). Normal B12, LFTs, serology, and spleen make common mimics unlikely, and diagnosis requires bone marrow examination.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old woman receiving induction chemotherapy for AML presents with fever 38.6°C and malaise. CBC shows ANC 0.2 ×10⁹/L. BP 102/62, HR 116, with no focal findings. Blood cultures are drawn. What is the best immediate management?
options: Immediate empiric IV antipseudomonal antibiotics (e.g. cefepime or piperacillin-tazobactam)
Wait for culture results before starting antibiotics
questions.explain: Fever with severe neutropenia is an emergency. Obtain cultures but do not delay empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics with antipseudomonal coverage. Vancomycin is added only for specific indications (line infection, pneumonia, skin/soft-tissue, instability, MRSA risk).

questions.stem: A 46-year-old man has progressive early satiety, LUQ fullness, night sweats, and 7 kg weight loss. The spleen is palpable 8 cm below the costal margin. CBC: WBC 145 ×10⁹/L, Hb 102 g/L, platelets 580 ×10⁹/L, with neutrophilia, a left shift, basophilia, and 2% blasts. Which test is most likely to confirm the diagnosis?
questions.explain: Marked leukocytosis with a neutrophilic left shift, basophilia, thrombocytosis, low blasts, and massive splenomegaly is classic chronic-phase CML, driven by the Philadelphia chromosome / BCR-ABL fusion.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man is found to have WBC 88 ×10⁹/L, Hb 133 g/L, platelets 190 ×10⁹/L, with lymphocytes 80 ×10⁹/L. He feels well aside from slowly enlarging painless cervical nodes; the film shows many mature lymphocytes and no blasts. Flow cytometry confirms CLL, and he has no B symptoms, cytopenias, bulky symptomatic nodes, or recurrent infections. What is the best initial management?
questions.explain: Asymptomatic early CLL is monitored, not treated. Therapy is reserved for symptomatic/progressive disease, marrow failure, bulky symptomatic nodes/spleen, B symptoms, rapid lymphocyte doubling, or refractory autoimmune cytopenias.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old student has 2 weeks of fever, severe fatigue, sore throat, and posterior cervical lymphadenopathy. Exam shows tonsillar exudates and mild splenomegaly; CBC shows lymphocytosis with atypical lymphocytes. She asks when she can return to rugby. Which advice is most appropriate?
options: Supportive care; avoid contact sports while splenomegaly is present (splenic rupture risk)
Start amoxicillin and return to rugby once afebrile
questions.explain: This is classic infectious mononucleosis (EBV). Care is supportive, and contact sports are avoided while the spleen is enlarged because of rupture risk. Amoxicillin/ampicillin can cause a characteristic rash in EBV and is not indicated.

questions.stem: A 63-year-old woman has back pain, fatigue, and recurrent respiratory infections. Labs: Hb 88 g/L, creatinine 185 µmol/L, calcium 3.0 mmol/L, elevated total protein; spine imaging shows lytic vertebral lesions. Which set of investigations best evaluates the suspected diagnosis?
options: Serum protein electrophoresis, serum free light chains, skeletal imaging, and bone marrow biopsy
BCR-ABL testing and abdominal ultrasound only
questions.explain: The CRAB features (hyperCalcemia, Renal dysfunction, Anemia, Bone lesions) suggest multiple myeloma. Workup: monoclonal protein testing (SPEP + immunofixation), serum free light chains, skeletal imaging, and bone marrow biopsy.

questions.stem: A 31-year-old man has an enlarging painless right supraclavicular node for 6 weeks, drenching night sweats, and 5 kg weight loss. The node is 3 cm and firm. CBC shows mild normocytic anemia, and chest X-ray shows mediastinal widening. What is the best next step?
options: Urgent imaging and tissue diagnosis, preferably a biopsy adequate for lymphoma architecture
Empiric oral antibiotics for 2 weeks with no further plan
Fine-needle aspiration only and no staging
Start prednisone before biopsy to shrink the node
questions.explain: A supraclavicular node, B symptoms, anemia, and mediastinal widening are concerning for lymphoma. Diagnosis needs adequate tissue (often excisional biopsy, since architecture matters). Steroids before biopsy can obscure the pathology and are generally avoided unless urgently needed for life-threatening compression.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman has Hb 165 g/L, hematocrit 0.53, platelets 520 ×10⁹/L, and WBC 13 ×10⁹/L. She reports itching after warm showers and burning redness in her hands. EPO is low and JAK2 mutation is detected. Which strategy best reduces her thrombosis risk?
options: Phlebotomy plus low-dose aspirin if no contraindication; cytoreduction based on risk
Observation only, as a high hemoglobin is protective
questions.explain: Elevated hematocrit with low EPO, JAK2 mutation, aquagenic pruritus, and erythromelalgia is polycythemia vera. Thrombosis risk is reduced with phlebotomy (target hematocrit) and low-dose aspirin, adding hydroxyurea cytoreduction for high-risk patients.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old woman has platelets of 980 ×10⁹/L on repeated CBCs with normal Hb and WBC. She has headaches and erythromelalgia, and workup supports essential thrombocythemia. She had a transient ischemic attack last year. Which treatment approach is most appropriate?
options: Low-dose aspirin plus cytoreduction (e.g. hydroxyurea), if no contraindications
No treatment, because platelets do not contribute to thrombosis
questions.explain: Essential thrombocythemia causes thrombotic/vasomotor symptoms. A prior TIA places her in a high-risk category, so low-dose aspirin plus cytoreduction (hydroxyurea) is appropriate. Watch hydroxyurea cytopenias/mucositis and aspirin bleeding.

questions.stem: A resident wants to study whether a new outpatient education program improves recognition of febrile-neutropenia warning symptoms among patients on chemotherapy. They define the population as adults receiving chemotherapy, the intervention as the program, the comparator as usual discharge teaching, and the outcome as correct identification of fever as an emergency. Which framework structures this quantitative question?
questions.explain: The question names a Population, Intervention, Comparator, and Outcome — the PICO structure. FINER instead judges whether a question is feasible/interesting/novel/ethical/relevant.

questions.stem: A 39-year-old man presents with fatigue, gum bleeding, and bruising. CBC shows pancytopenia with circulating promyelocytes; coagulation studies show prolonged PT/PTT, low fibrinogen, and elevated D-dimer. The team suspects acute promyelocytic leukemia. What is the most appropriate immediate step?
options: Start ATRA urgently while confirming the diagnosis
Withhold all therapy until cytogenetics return
Begin heparin infusion as primary therapy
Start a BCR-ABL tyrosine kinase inhibitor
questions.explain: APL carries life-threatening DIC. When suspected, start ATRA urgently while confirming the diagnosis — early ATRA reduces early hemorrhagic death. Watch for differentiation syndrome (fever, infiltrates, edema, hypotension), treated with steroids.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old man with a high-burden, rapidly proliferating ALL is about to start induction. He is from a region where G6PD deficiency is common, and screening confirms G6PD deficiency. He is at high risk for tumor lysis syndrome. Which urate-lowering approach is most appropriate?
options: Allopurinol with aggressive IV hydration (avoid rasburicase)
Rasburicase, the preferred agent regardless of G6PD status
No prophylaxis; treat hyperkalemia only if it occurs
questions.explain: Rasburicase causes hemolysis and methemoglobinemia in G6PD deficiency and must be avoided. Use allopurinol with aggressive hydration and electrolyte monitoring for tumor-lysis prevention in this patient.

questions.stem: A 74-year-old man with lower-risk MDS has symptomatic transfusion-dependent anemia, and cytogenetics show an isolated del(5q) abnormality. He is not a transplant candidate. Which disease-modifying therapy is classically effective in this subgroup?
questions.explain: Lenalidomide is classically effective in del(5q) MDS, often reducing transfusion dependence. Monitor for cytopenias, thrombosis, and teratogenicity. Hypomethylating agents (azacitidine/decitabine) are used for higher-risk disease.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman started on an antithyroid drug for Graves disease develops a fever and sore throat 6 weeks later. CBC shows an ANC of 0.3 ×10⁹/L with otherwise normal lines. Which is the most likely explanation?
options: Drug-induced agranulocytosis from the antithyroid medication
questions.explain: Antithyroid drugs (methimazole, propylthiouracil) are classic causes of drug-induced agranulocytosis, which often presents with fever/sore throat. Stop the drug and manage as febrile neutropenia. Clozapine, sulfonamides, and carbamazepine are other culprits.

questions.stem: A blood film on a patient with marked leukocytosis is reported as showing a neutrophilic left shift with prominent basophilia. Which diagnosis does the basophilia most specifically suggest?
questions.explain: Basophils rarely drive leukocytosis, so basophilia is a key clue for CML, especially alongside a neutrophilic left shift and splenomegaly. Confirm with BCR-ABL testing.

questions.stem: Which lymph node finding is most concerning for malignancy and should prompt biopsy?
options: A hard, fixed supraclavicular node larger than 2 cm
A 1 cm tender mobile node during a sore throat
A soft submandibular node with dental infection
A small reactive node that shrinks over 2 weeks
A tender inguinal node after a foot laceration
questions.explain: Hard, fixed, supraclavicular, and >2 cm are classic malignancy red flags warranting biopsy. Tender, soft, mobile nodes that resolve, or that drain an obvious local infection, are usually reactive.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man has a spleen palpable into the left iliac fossa, anemia, and a blood film showing teardrop cells and a leukoerythroblastic picture. Which diagnosis best fits this massive splenomegaly?
questions.explain: Massive splenomegaly with teardrop cells and a leukoerythroblastic film (extramedullary hematopoiesis from marrow fibrosis) is characteristic of primary myelofibrosis. CML, hairy cell leukemia, and some lymphomas are other massive-spleen causes.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man with a large anterior mediastinal mass and suspected lymphoma is orthopneic and has facial plethora with distended neck veins. The team plans a diagnostic biopsy. Which management caution is most important?
options: Avoid unnecessary sedation or general anesthesia because of airway/SVC compromise
Proceed directly to general anesthesia for an open biopsy
Start empiric chemotherapy before any tissue diagnosis
Give a large IV fluid bolus to treat the facial swelling
Discharge with outpatient follow-up in 4 weeks
questions.explain: An anterior mediastinal mass can compress the airway and great vessels (SVC syndrome). Sedation/general anesthesia can precipitate airway or cardiovascular collapse, so they are avoided when possible; urgent specialist involvement is needed. Steroids before biopsy can obscure pathology unless life-threatening.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man with Hodgkin lymphoma is to receive ABVD chemotherapy. He is a competitive swimmer and asks about long-term lung effects. Which component of ABVD is most associated with pulmonary toxicity?
questions.explain: Bleomycin causes pulmonary toxicity (pneumonitis/fibrosis), with caution around lung disease and high inspired oxygen. Doxorubicin causes cardiomyopathy, vinblastine neuropathy, and dacarbazine nausea/myelosuppression. (Rituximab is not part of ABVD.)

questions.stem: A student has drafted a structured clinical question and now wants to appraise whether it is worth pursuing — considering whether it can realistically be done, whether it adds new knowledge, and whether it is ethical. Which framework is she applying at this stage?
questions.explain: FINER (Feasible, Interesting, Novel, Ethical, Relevant) appraises whether a question is worth pursuing. PICO is used earlier to build the structure of the question; the two are complementary, not interchangeable.

cards.prompt: What are the first two questions to ask about any abnormal CBC?
cards.answer: Which cell line(s) are abnormal, and is it one line or several? Multiple lines down points to a marrow (factory) problem until proven otherwise.

cards.prompt: Why do blasts in a pancytopenic patient change the urgency?
cards.answer: Blasts shift the differential to acute leukemia, MDS, or myelofibrosis — a marrow-production/infiltration problem, not an isolated anemia — and prompt urgent hematology and bone marrow examination.

cards.prompt: Why does MDS cause cytopenias despite a cellular marrow?
cards.answer: The clonal stem cells produce dysplastic progeny that function poorly and undergo apoptosis — ineffective hematopoiesis — so peripheral counts fall even though the marrow looks cellular.

cards.prompt: Why is febrile neutropenia a medical emergency?
cards.answer: Neutropenic patients can have life-threatening infection with few localizing signs and may deteriorate rapidly, so empiric broad-spectrum antipseudomonal antibiotics are started immediately after cultures — without waiting for results.

cards.prompt: What genetic lesion drives CML, and what is first-line therapy?
cards.answer: The Philadelphia chromosome produces the BCR-ABL fusion tyrosine kinase; first-line therapy is a BCR-ABL tyrosine kinase inhibitor (e.g. imatinib, dasatinib, nilotinib).

cards.prompt: When is asymptomatic CLL treated rather than monitored?
cards.answer: Treat for B symptoms, progressive symptomatic lymphadenopathy/splenomegaly, marrow failure (anemia/thrombocytopenia), rapid lymphocyte doubling, or autoimmune cytopenias refractory to standard therapy. Otherwise, watchful waiting.

cards.prompt: What does CRAB stand for in multiple myeloma?
cards.answer: hyperCalcemia, Renal dysfunction, Anemia, and Bone lesions — the end-organ damage that defines symptomatic myeloma.

cards.prompt: What are the two classic management pitfalls in infectious mononucleosis?
cards.answer: Avoid contact sports while splenomegaly is present (splenic rupture risk), and avoid amoxicillin/ampicillin (causes a characteristic rash in EBV infection).

cards.prompt: Why is ATRA started urgently when APL is suspected?
cards.answer: APL causes severe DIC with a high risk of early fatal hemorrhage; starting ATRA promptly (before full confirmation) reduces early death by driving promyelocyte differentiation.

cards.prompt: List classic causes of MASSIVE splenomegaly.
cards.answer: CML, primary myelofibrosis, hairy cell leukemia, some lymphomas, and chronic infections/parasitic disease in the right epidemiologic context.

cards.text: Pancytopenia means a fall in all three lines: {{anemia}}, leukopenia/neutropenia, and {{thrombocytopenia}}.
cards.explain: Recognizing all three lines down reframes the problem as marrow disease.

cards.text: AML arises from the {{myeloid}} lineage; ALL arises from the {{lymphoid}} lineage and especially requires {{CNS}} prophylaxis with intrathecal chemotherapy.
cards.explain: Lineage is defined by flow cytometry; CNS prophylaxis is a hallmark of ALL treatment.

cards.text: Chronic-phase CML shows marked leukocytosis with a neutrophilic {{left shift}} and {{basophilia}}, confirmed by {{BCR-ABL}} testing.
cards.explain: Basophilia plus splenomegaly is the classic clue; BCR-ABL confirms.

cards.text: Polycythemia vera shows a raised hematocrit with a {{low}} EPO and a {{JAK2}} mutation in most cases.
cards.explain: Low EPO with high red cell mass distinguishes primary PV from secondary erythrocytosis.

cards.text: Empiric therapy for febrile neutropenia must cover {{Pseudomonas}}; add {{vancomycin}} only for specific indications such as line infection or instability.
cards.explain: Antipseudomonal beta-lactam first; vancomycin is not automatic.

cards.text: PICO structures a quantitative question as Population, {{Intervention}}, Comparator, and {{Outcome}}.
cards.explain: PICO builds the question; FINER judges whether it is worth doing.

cards.text: Neutropenia is severe (high infection risk) when the ANC is below {{0.5}} ×10⁹/L and profound below {{0.1}} ×10⁹/L.
cards.explain: ANC thresholds stratify infection risk.

cards.text: Myeloma workup includes serum protein electrophoresis with {{immunofixation}}, serum {{free light chains}}, skeletal imaging, and a bone marrow biopsy.
cards.explain: These identify and quantify the monoclonal protein and clonal plasma cells.

cards.prompt: Which agent is classically used in del(5q) MDS?
cards.explain: Lenalidomide is classically effective in del(5q) MDS; hypomethylating agents are used for higher-risk disease.

cards.prompt: Which urate-lowering drug must be avoided in G6PD deficiency?
cards.explain: Rasburicase causes hemolysis/methemoglobinemia in G6PD deficiency; use allopurinol with hydration instead.

cards.prompt: Which presentation suggests leukostasis in acute leukemia?
options: Dyspnea, hypoxia, and neurologic/visual symptoms with very high blasts
cards.explain: Very high blast counts can sludge the microcirculation, causing respiratory and neurologic compromise that needs urgent cytoreduction.

cards.prompt: Which test confirms the immunophenotype of CLL?
cards.explain: Flow cytometry confirms a clonal mature B-cell population; node biopsy is reserved for suspected transformation.

cards.prompt: Which serious early risk requires a dose ramp-up when starting venetoclax for CLL?
cards.explain: Venetoclax can precipitate tumor lysis syndrome, so it is started with a dose ramp-up and TLS precautions.

cards.prompt: Why is excisional biopsy often preferred for suspected lymphoma?
options: It preserves nodal architecture needed for classification
It is faster than fine-needle aspiration
cards.explain: Lymphoma classification depends on nodal architecture, which an excisional biopsy preserves better than FNA/core.

cards.prompt: Which ABVD agent is most associated with pulmonary toxicity?
cards.explain: Bleomycin causes pneumonitis/fibrosis; doxorubicin is cardiotoxic, vinblastine neurotoxic, dacarbazine emetogenic.

cards.prompt: How does MGUS differ from multiple myeloma?
options: MGUS has a monoclonal protein but no myeloma-defining end-organ damage
MGUS always has lytic bone lesions
MGUS has higher clonal burden than smoldering myeloma
cards.explain: MGUS is premalignant: a monoclonal protein without CRAB/myeloma-defining events; smoldering myeloma has higher burden but still no end-organ damage.

cards.prompt: Which adverse effect is characteristic of bortezomib?
cards.explain: Bortezomib commonly causes peripheral neuropathy and zoster reactivation, so antiviral prophylaxis is often used.

cards.prompt: Which CBC/film findings should prompt urgent concern? (select all)
cards.explain: Blasts, pancytopenia, and severe febrile neutropenia are danger signs; a stable mild normocytic anemia is not urgent.

cards.prompt: Which conditions cause multiple cytopenias (multilineage disease)? (select all)
cards.explain: Leukemia, MDS, and severe B12/folate deficiency hit multiple lines; isolated iron deficiency causes anemia alone.

cards.prompt: Which are recognized emergencies in acute leukemia? (select all)
cards.explain: Febrile neutropenia, tumor lysis, and leukostasis are leukemia emergencies; aquagenic pruritus is a polycythemia vera symptom.

cards.prompt: Which drugs are classic causes of neutropenia/agranulocytosis? (select all)
cards.explain: Clozapine, antithyroid drugs, and sulfonamides/TMP-SMX are classic culprits; aspirin is not a typical cause.

cards.prompt: Which node features raise concern for malignancy? (select all)
options: Tender and mobile during a sore throat
cards.explain: Supraclavicular, hard/fixed, and >2 cm are red flags; tender mobile nodes with a concurrent infection are usually reactive.

cards.prompt: Which are the B symptoms of lymphoma? (select all)
cards.explain: B symptoms are fever, drenching night sweats, and unintentional weight loss; local tenderness suggests a reactive node.

cards.prompt: Order the immediate management of febrile neutropenia:
items: Assess ABCs / sepsis physiology and vitals
Add vancomycin / antifungals only if specifically indicated
cards.explain: Stabilize, culture, give antipseudomonal abx without delay, then escalate only for specific indications.

cards.prompt: Order the structured approach to an abnormal CBC:
items: Identify which cell line(s) are abnormal
Decide if it is isolated or multilineage
cards.explain: Line → isolated vs multilineage → stability → differential.

cards.prompt: Order a sensible workup for suspected multiple myeloma:
items: Basic labs: CBC, calcium, creatinine, albumin
Serum protein electrophoresis + immunofixation and free light chains
Bone marrow biopsy to quantify clonal plasma cells
cards.explain: Screen with basics, characterize the monoclonal protein, image bone, then confirm with marrow.

cards.prompt: Order the approach to persistent lymphadenopathy:
items: History and exam for red flags
CBC + film and targeted labs (HIV, EBV, ESR/CRP)
Imaging to assess extent if malignancy suspected
Excisional biopsy when red flags are present
cards.explain: Characterize clinically, screen with labs, image, then biopsy if concerning.

cards.prompt: Match the leukemia to its hallmark:
pairs: Mature lymphocytosis, painless nodes, watchful waiting
cards.explain: Tempo, dominant cell, and genetic clue separate the leukemias.

cards.prompt: Match the leukocytosis pattern to a typical cause:
cards.explain: The differential subtype directs the differential diagnosis.

cards.prompt: Match the myeloproliferative neoplasm to a management mainstay:
cards.explain: Each MPN has a characteristic first-line strategy.

cards.prompt: Match the chemotherapy agent to its signature toxicity:
cards.explain: These agent-specific toxicities recur across leukemia/lymphoma regimens.

cards.prompt: Match the plasma-cell condition to its concept:
pairs: Clonal malignancy with CRAB / defining biomarkers
cards.explain: The spectrum runs from premalignant MGUS to symptomatic myeloma.

cards.prompt: Match the research framework to its purpose:
pairs: Judge whether a question is worth doing
cards.explain: PICO structures; FINER appraises.

cards.prompt: What does G-CSF do, and what are its adverse effects?
cards.answer: Granulocyte colony-stimulating factor stimulates neutrophil production (used prophylactically/therapeutically, especially in chemotherapy-related neutropenia). Adverse effects: bone pain, leukocytosis, and rare splenic rupture.

cards.prompt: Name a key safety concern for nilotinib and for ponatinib.
cards.answer: Nilotinib can cause QT prolongation and arterial vascular events; ponatinib carries a notable risk of arterial and venous thrombosis (and pancreatitis/hypertension).

cards.prompt: Why is sedation/general anesthesia risky with a large anterior mediastinal mass?
cards.answer: The mass can compress the airway and great vessels (including the SVC); loss of airway tone or preload under sedation/GA can precipitate airway or cardiovascular collapse, so they are avoided when possible.

cards.prompt: What do qualitative research questions typically explore?
cards.answer: Experiences, meaning, barriers, facilitators, and context — e.g. patients' experiences of prolonged leukemia treatment or perceived barriers to choosing watchful waiting in CLL.

guides.title: Week 16 · 1 Reading a Dangerous CBC
guides.html:
Approach

Reading a Dangerous CBC

Week 16 · Blood III
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

Which counts should scare you, isolated vs multilineage, and the unstable physiology to catch first.

CBC findings that should worry you

  • Pancytopenia — anemia + leukopenia/neutropenia + thrombocytopenia.
  • Blasts in the peripheral blood — acute leukemia until proven otherwise.
  • Severe neutropenia, especially with fever.
  • Very high WBC with immature granulocytes, basophilia, splenomegaly, or constitutional symptoms.
  • Multiple abnormal cell lines — shifts thinking to marrow disease.
  • Marked lymphocytosis in an older adult with nodes → CLL.
  • Macrocytosis + cytopenias + dysplastic neutrophils → MDS.
You aren't expected to be a hematopathologist — you ARE expected to recognize when a CBC pattern is dangerous and needs a film, a marrow, or urgent hematology.

Step 1 — which line is abnormal?

LineLowHigh
RBCAnemiaPolycythemia (hyperviscosity/thrombosis)
NeutrophilNeutropenia (infection)Neutrophilia (infection/inflam/MPN)
LymphocyteLymphopeniaLymphocytosis (virus, CLL)
PlateletThrombocytopenia (bleed)Thrombocytosis (reactive vs ET)

Step 2 — isolated or multilineage?

An isolated abnormality allows a focused approach. Multiple abnormal lines raise concern for acute leukemia, MDS, myelofibrosis, aplastic anemia, marrow infiltration, severe B12/folate deficiency, hypersplenism, or severe systemic illness.

Step 3 — is the patient unstable?

Urgent physiology — look before the differential
Fever with neutropenia; sepsis (hypotension, tachycardia, confusion); severe symptomatic anemia (chest pain, syncope, heart failure); bleeding with severe thrombocytopenia; leukostasis (dyspnea, hypoxia, neuro/visual symptoms with very high blasts).

First two questions for any abnormal CBC

Which line(s) are abnormal, and is it one line or several? Several lines down points to a marrow (factory) problem until proven otherwise.

guides.title: Week 16 · 2 Pancytopenia & MDS
guides.html:
Marrow failure

Pancytopenia & MDS

Week 16 · Blood III
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

When all three lines fall: the workup that matters, and the dysplastic clonal disorder of older adults.

Pancytopenia

Reduction in all three lines (anemia + leukopenia/neutropenia + thrombocytopenia). With blasts, the differential narrows to acute leukemia, MDS, or myelofibrosis. The key move: this is a marrow-production/infiltration problem, not an isolated anemia.

DeficitConsequence
AnemiaFatigue, pallor, exertional dyspnea, demand ischemia
NeutropeniaFever may be the only sign; serious bacterial/fungal infection
ThrombocytopeniaPetechiae, mucosal bleeding; intracranial hemorrhage if profound

Initial workup

Repeat CBC + differential, blood film, reticulocytes, B12/folate, liver and renal tests, HIV/hepatitis when appropriate, hemolysis screen (bilirubin, LDH, haptoglobin, DAT) if indicated, abdominal US for splenomegaly, and bone marrow aspirate + biopsy when malignancy/MDS/aplastic anemia/infiltration is suspected.

Myelodysplastic syndrome

A clonal hematopoietic stem-cell disorder of older adults: the clone fills the marrow but its progeny are dysplastic and die early → ineffective hematopoiesis and cytopenias despite a cellular marrow.

ClueDetail
Macrocytic anemiaOval macrocytes; one or more cytopenias
Dysplastic neutrophilsHypolobated / hypogranular forms
BlastsMay be present but below the AML threshold
Diagnosis & course
Requires bone marrow examination (trilineage dysplasia, increased blasts below AML threshold). About one-third progress to AML; cytopenias themselves drive infection/bleeding morbidity.

Management

Risk-adapted. Supportive: transfusions, antibiotics, ESAs, iron chelation in chronically transfused patients. Disease-modifying: hypomethylating agents (azacitidine, decitabine) for higher risk; lenalidomide for del(5q); allogeneic transplant is the only cure but limited by age/comorbidity. Watch lenalidomide thrombosis/teratogenicity and azacitidine myelosuppression.

guides.title: Week 16 · 3 Acute Leukemia & Its Emergencies
guides.html:
Oncologic emergency

Acute Leukemia & Its Emergencies

Week 16 · Blood III
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

AML vs ALL, the marrow-failure presentation, and the four emergencies that kill before the diagnosis is final.

AML vs ALL

FeatureAMLALL
LineageMyeloidLymphoid
AgeAdults/olderChildren > adults
TreatmentInduction → consolidation/transplantInduction + CNS prophylaxis (intrathecal)

Both present with marrow failure: anemia, neutropenia (fever/infection even if WBC high, since blasts are nonfunctional), thrombocytopenia, bone pain. Lymphadenopathy/hepatosplenomegaly favor ALL.

Investigations

Smear, marrow aspirate + biopsy, flow cytometry (lineage), cytogenetics/molecular (prognosis, targets), coagulation (if APL suspected), tumor-lysis labs, infection workup if febrile.

Four emergencies

Febrile neutropenia
Immediate empiric broad-spectrum antipseudomonal antibiotics; do not wait for cultures.
Tumor lysis syndrome
High burden of proliferating cells. Hydration + monitor K/PO₄/Ca/urate/Cr. Allopurinol for prevention in lower risk; rasburicase for high risk/established — but rasburicase causes hemolysis/methemoglobinemia in G6PD deficiency, so avoid it there.
Leukostasis
Very high blasts → dyspnea, hypoxia, headache, confusion, visual/stroke-like signs. Urgent cytoreduction.
Acute promyelocytic leukemia (APL)
AML subtype with severe DIC/bleeding. If suspected, start ATRA urgently while confirming. ATRA → differentiation syndrome (fever, infiltrates, edema, hypotension) treated with steroids.

Treatment toxicities to know

Myelosuppression/infection, mucositis, anthracycline cardiotoxicity, vincristine neuropathy/constipation, steroid hyperglycemia, asparaginase pancreatitis/thrombosis, intrathecal neurotoxicity.

guides.title: Week 16 · 4 Leukocytosis & Neutropenia
guides.html:
WBC

Leukocytosis & Neutropenia

Week 16 · Blood III
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

Sort a high WBC by subtype, recognize reactive neutrophilia, and run the febrile-neutropenia emergency.

Leukocytosis — which subtype?

PatternReactive causesClonal concern
NeutrophiliaInfection, inflammation, stress, steroids, smokingCML
LymphocytosisVirus, pertussisCLL
MonocytosisInfection, autoimmune, cirrhosisCMML
EosinophiliaAllergy, asthma, parasites, drugsHypereosinophilic syndrome
Basophils rarely drive a high WBC — but basophilia is a key clue for CML.

Refer urgently if blasts, marked leukocytosis, cytopenias, constitutional symptoms, or organomegaly are present; otherwise repeat the CBC if mild and likely reactive.

Neutropenia by ANC

ANC (×10⁹/L)Risk
1.0–1.5Mild — often low risk
0.5–1.0Moderate
<0.5Severe — high infection risk
<0.1Profound — very high risk

Drug causes to know: chemotherapy, clozapine, antithyroid drugs (methimazole, PTU), sulfonamides/TMP-SMX, carbamazepine.

Febrile neutropenia — an emergency

Immediate management
Fever may be the only sign. Assess ABCs/sepsis → blood cultures (peripheral + any line) → start empiric broad-spectrum antipseudomonal antibiotics without delay (piperacillin-tazobactam, cefepime, or a carbapenem). Add vancomycin only for line infection, skin/soft-tissue, pneumonia, instability, or MRSA risk. Consider antifungals if fever persists in prolonged neutropenia.

G-CSF stimulates neutrophil production (prophylactic/therapeutic in selected patients); adverse effects: bone pain, leukocytosis, rare splenic rupture.

guides.title: Week 16 · 5 Chronic Leukemias & MPNs
guides.html:
Chronic

Chronic Leukemias & MPNs

Week 16 · Blood III
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

CML and its BCR-ABL TKIs, indolent CLL and watchful waiting, and the JAK2-driven myeloproliferative trio.

Chronic myeloid leukemia (CML)

An MPN driven by the Philadelphia chromosome → BCR-ABL fusion tyrosine kinase. Often found incidentally; may give fatigue, weight loss, night sweats, and LUQ fullness from splenomegaly.

FilmConfirmTreat
Marked leukocytosis, neutrophilic left shift, basophilia, ↑platelets, few blastsBCR-ABL molecular/cytogeneticsTKI: imatinib, dasatinib, nilotinib, bosutinib, ponatinib

TKI cautions: nilotinib QT prolongation/arterial events, dasatinib pleural effusion, ponatinib arterial/venous thrombosis.

Chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL)

A mature B-cell disorder of older adults, often indolent over years. Painless lymphadenopathy, fatigue, recurrent infection; autoimmune hemolytic anemia/ITP can occur. Film: mature lymphocytosis, no blasts; confirm by flow cytometry.

Watchful waiting
Asymptomatic early CLL is monitored, not treated. Treat for B symptoms, progressive symptomatic adenopathy/splenomegaly, marrow failure, rapid lymphocyte doubling, or uncontrolled autoimmune cytopenias. Options: BTK inhibitors (ibrutinib), BCL-2 inhibitor (venetoclax — TLS risk, dose ramp), anti-CD20 (rituximab).

The other MPNs

DiseaseClueManagement
Essential thrombocythemiaPersistent thrombocytosis; JAK2/CALR/MPLLow-dose ASA; hydroxyurea if high risk
Polycythemia vera↑Hct, low EPO, JAK2, aquagenic pruritus, erythromelalgiaPhlebotomy + low-dose ASA; hydroxyurea if high risk
Primary myelofibrosisMassive splenomegaly, teardrop cells, leukoerythroblastic filmSupportive; ruxolitinib; transplant in selected

guides.title: Week 16 · 6 Lymphadenopathy & Lymphoma
guides.html:
Lymphoid

Lymphadenopathy & Lymphoma

Week 16 · Blood III
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

Read the node, find the red flags, biopsy correctly, and separate indolent from aggressive lymphoma.

Sizing up a node

Nodes are generally abnormal if >1 cm (inguinal often 1.5–2 cm). Pattern matters: localized (local infection, metastatic carcinoma, lymphoma) vs generalized (virus, HIV, autoimmune, lymphoma/leukemia, drugs).

FeatureInterpretation
Tender, softMore likely reactive/inflammatory
RubberyBenign or lymphoma
Hard, fixedConcerning for carcinoma
>2 cm, supraclavicular, generalizedConcerning for malignancy/systemic disease
When to biopsy — and how
Biopsy for persistent unexplained nodes, >2 cm, supraclavicular, hard/fixed, B symptoms, generalized + abnormal labs, or a mediastinal mass. For suspected lymphoma, excisional biopsy is preferred — nodal architecture matters. Avoid steroids before biopsy (obscures pathology).

Lymphoma: indolent vs aggressive

IndolentAggressive
Slow (years); waxing/waning nodesRapid (weeks); B symptoms, organ compromise
Follicular lymphomaDiffuse large B-cell lymphoma (R-CHOP)
Observe if asymptomaticTreat promptly with curative intent

B symptoms: fever, drenching night sweats, unintentional weight loss.

Hodgkin lymphoma

Painless cervical/supraclavicular nodes ± mediastinal mass; staged with PET/CT; treated with ABVD. Toxicities: doxorubicin cardiomyopathy, bleomycin pulmonary toxicity, vinblastine neuropathy.

Mediastinal mass — urgent
May compress airway/great vessels/SVC. Avoid unnecessary sedation/general anesthesia with airway compression; urgent specialist input for SVC syndrome or stridor.

guides.title: Week 16 · 7 Myeloma, Mononucleosis & Splenomegaly
guides.html:
Plasma cell + spleen

Myeloma, Mononucleosis & Splenomegaly

Week 16 · Blood III
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

CRAB and the myeloma workup, the EBV pitfalls, and how to think about a big spleen.

Plasma cell disorders

ConditionConcept
MGUSMonoclonal protein, no end-organ damage; premalignant
Smoldering myelomaHigher clonal burden, no myeloma-defining events
Multiple myelomaClonal plasma-cell malignancy with end-organ damage
CRAB: Calcium ↑ (confusion, constipation, polyuria), Renal injury (light chains), Anemia, Bone lesions (pain, lytic, fractures). Clues: recurrent infection, ↑ESR, rouleaux.

Workup: CBC, calcium, creatinine, albumin; serum protein electrophoresis + immunofixation; serum free light chains; urine studies; skeletal imaging (whole-body low-dose CT/PET/MRI); bone marrow biopsy. Therapy classes: proteasome inhibitor (bortezomib — neuropathy, zoster), IMiD (lenalidomide — thrombosis), dexamethasone, daratumumab, autologous transplant, bisphosphonates (jaw osteonecrosis). Emergencies: hypercalcemia, AKI, cord compression.

Infectious mononucleosis

Fever, pharyngitis with exudates, fatigue, posterior cervical nodes, splenomegaly, atypical lymphocytosis; heterophile/Monospot (limited early), EBV serology, mildly elevated LFTs.

Two classic pitfalls
Avoid contact sports while splenomegaly persists (splenic rupture); avoid amoxicillin/ampicillin (causes a characteristic rash in EBV). Care is supportive; steroids only for severe complications (airway, severe cytopenias).

Splenomegaly

The spleen clears senescent cells, presents antigen, and can host extramedullary hematopoiesis. Hypersplenism → cytopenias. Massive splenomegaly: CML, primary myelofibrosis, hairy cell leukemia, some lymphomas, chronic infection. Workup: CBC + film, LFTs, infectious testing, abdominal US; refer if massive/unexplained or with cytopenias/B symptoms. Counsel against contact sports/abdominal trauma.

guides.title: Week 16 · 8 Formulating a Research Question
guides.html:
Scholarship

Formulating a Research Question

Week 16 · Blood III
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

Where questions come from, and the PICO and FINER frameworks for building and judging them.

What is a research question?

A clear, focused question the study is designed to answer. It specifies what is examined and keeps the project scope manageable.

Where questions come from

Clinical experience, practice gaps, accepted ideas needing testing, conflicting views, different disciplinary perspectives, curiosity/preclinical work, and informed imagination.

PICO — build a quantitative question

LetterElement
PPatient / population / problem
IIntervention / exposure
CComparator
OOutcome
Example: In adults with newly diagnosed CML, does first-line dasatinib compared with imatinib improve molecular response at 12 months?

FINER — judge the question

LetterCriterion
FFeasible
IInteresting
NNovel
EEthical
RRelevant
PICO vs FINER — don't confuse them
PICO builds the structure of a quantitative question; FINER judges whether that question is worth doing. They are complementary, not interchangeable.

Qualitative questions

Focus on experiences, meaning, barriers, facilitators, and context — e.g. "What barriers do older adults with CLL perceive when deciding between watchful waiting and treatment?"

guides.title: Week 16 · 9 Cram Sheet
guides.html:
Night-Before High-Yield

Blood III

Week 16 · Blood III
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite
★ Do-not-miss · first-step reflexes
Febrile neutropenia (fever + ANC <0.5) → empiric broad-spectrum antipseudomonal antibiotics now — don't wait for cultures
Acute promyelocytic leukemia (promyelocytes + DIC) → start ATRA urgently
Tumor lysis syndrome → hydrate + rasburicase; avoid rasburicase in G6PD → allopurinol
Leukostasis (very high blasts, dyspnea/neuro) → urgent cytoreduction
TTP (schistocytes + low platelets, normal coags) → plasma exchange; do NOT transfuse platelets
Anterior mediastinal mass + SVC/airway signs → avoid sedation/GA; urgent specialist
Myeloma: cord compression or symptomatic hyperCa → urgent steroids + imaging / IV saline
Blasts on the smear → acute leukemia until proven otherwise — urgent heme + marrow

Reading a dangerous CBC Approach

Blasts acute leukemia until proven otherwise
Pancytopenia marrow problem (leukemia / MDS / MF / aplastic)
Multiple lines down marrow disease, not isolated
First questions which line(s)? one or several?
Urgent febrile neutropenia, leukostasis, severe anemia, bleeding

Pancytopenia & MDS MDS

Pancytopenia + blasts AL / MDS / myelofibrosis → marrow
MDS clonal, older adult; macrocytic anemia + dysplastic neutrophils
MDS mechanism ineffective hematopoiesis (cellular marrow, low counts)
MDS Dx marrow: trilineage dysplasia, blasts < AML threshold
~1/3 MDS progress to AML
del(5q) MDS lenalidomide
Higher-risk MDS azacitidine / decitabine
Only cure allogeneic transplant

Acute leukemia AML/ALL

AML vs ALL myeloid vs lymphoid; ALL needs CNS prophylaxis
Presentation marrow failure: anemia, neutropenia, thrombocytopenia
Dx marrow + flow cytometry + cytogenetics
APL DIC → start ATRA urgently (differentiation syndrome → steroids)
Tumor lysis hydrate; allopurinol (prevent) / rasburicase (high-risk)
Rasburicase contraindicated in G6PD
Febrile neutropenia empiric antipseudomonal antibiotics

Leukocytosis & neutropenia WBC

Neutrophilia infection, inflammation, steroids, smoking, CML
Lymphocytosis virus, pertussis, CLL
Basophilia CML clue
Monocytosis / eosinophilia CMML / allergy-parasites
ANC <0.5 / <0.1 severe / profound neutropenia
Drug neutropenia clozapine, antithyroid (methimazole/PTU), sulfonamides
G-CSF stimulates neutrophils (bone pain)

Chronic leukemias & MPNs Chronic

CML BCR-ABL, basophilia, left shift, splenomegaly → TKI
TKI cautions nilotinib QT/arterial; dasatinib pleural effusion; ponatinib thrombosis
CLL mature lymphocytosis, painless nodes; flow confirms
CLL early / asx watchful waiting
CLL drugs ibrutinib (afib/bleeding); venetoclax (TLS, dose ramp)
ET thrombocytosis; ASA ± hydroxyurea
PV ↑Hct, low EPO, JAK2, aquagenic pruritus → phlebotomy + ASA
PMF teardrops, massive spleen, leukoerythroblastic → ruxolitinib

Lymphadenopathy & lymphoma Lymphoid

Node red flags hard/fixed, >2 cm, supraclavicular, B symptoms, generalized
Biopsy excisional (architecture); avoid steroids pre-biopsy
B symptoms fever, drenching night sweats, weight loss
Hodgkin cervical/supraclavicular; ABVD
Bleomycin pulmonary toxicity
DLBCL (aggressive) R-CHOP; vincristine neuropathy, doxorubicin cardiotox
Mediastinal mass avoid sedation/GA (airway / SVC)

Myeloma · mono · spleen Plasma

Multiple myeloma CRAB: Ca↑, Renal, Anemia, Bone lytic
Workup SPEP + immunofixation, free light chains, skeletal imaging, marrow
MGUS M-protein, no end-organ damage (premalignant)
Bortezomib neuropathy + zoster (prophylaxis)
Mono (EBV) posterior nodes, atypical lymphocytes
EBV pitfalls avoid amoxicillin (rash) + contact sports (splenic rupture)
Massive spleen CML, PMF, hairy cell, lymphoma

Research module Scholarship

PICO Population, Intervention, Comparator, Outcome — builds the question
FINER Feasible, Interesting, Novel, Ethical, Relevant — judges it
Qualitative experiences, barriers, meaning
Week 16 · Blood III · cram sheet · cue → answer

facets.adverse: Edema, nausea, muscle cramps, cytopenias, rash
QT prolongation, arterial vascular events, metabolic effects
facets.contra: Caution with QT-prolonging drugs / cardiac risk

facets.adverse: Differentiation syndrome (fever, infiltrates, edema, hypotension) → steroids; headache, dry mucosa
Cytopenias, mouth ulcers, skin/nail changes; teratogenic
Tumor lysis syndrome (requires dose ramp-up), neutropenia
Infusion reactions, hepatitis B reactivation, infections
Infusion reactions; interferes with blood crossmatch

facets.contra: Caution: lung disease, high inspired O2

facets.adverse: Bone pain, leukocytosis, rare splenic rupture
Neurotoxicity (esp. renal impairment), allergy, C. difficile

facets.use: Add for specific neutropenic-fever indications (line infx, MRSA, instability)
Low-dose in ET / PV to reduce thrombosis
facets.adverse: GI bleeding, bronchospasm in sensitive asthmatics
